content: The night at Jemaa el-Fnaa is vibrant but can be low in light. Keep your shutter speed above 1/60 and include smoky street food stalls in the foreground to add dynamic composition.
The snail soup (Babbouche) at stall number 14 in the night market is highly recommended by locals. It has strong spices, so pairing it with mint tea is a great choice.
The cobalt blue walls of Majorelle Garden look most vivid between 9 and 10 AM. Use the sunlight filtering through cactus paths for beautiful portrait shots.
The bench near the small pond inside the garden is one of the quietest spots in Marrakech. Perfect for meditation or writing a travel journal while listening to the water.
Bahia Palace, meaning 'brilliance,' showcases the finest Moroccan architecture of the 19th century. Explore the rooms and compare the intricate tile patterns (Zellij).
The 70-meter Koutoubia minaret is visible from almost anywhere in Marrakech. By law, no building can be taller, preserving the city's skyline.
Capture symmetry in the central courtyard of the madrasa. The carved cedar wood and marble floor reflections enhance the geometric beauty.
Souk Semmarine is the largest market in Marrakech. When buying leather goods, start bargaining at one-third of the asking price. Look out for babouche shoes and lanterns.
Al Fassia is a traditional Moroccan restaurant run entirely by female chefs. The lamb and prune tagine offers a perfect balance of sweet and savory flavors.
A hidden garden completely isolated from the noise of the market. Enjoy Moroccan mint tea on the rooftop terrace while overlooking the city.
